strategy: agentic chunking via Gemini (headline + summary + original text)

pages.text: HOW TO SAVE TIME IN THE GYM

2

Sometimes it's difficult to free up enough time in your schedule to complete all your
training sessions as planned. Here are 4 tips to save time in the gym in the best order to
apply them.

The central principle of time saving is to stimulate your muscles as well as possible in
the time that you have. Since muscle growth correlates tightly with training volume, time
saving techniques often come down to strategies to condense training volume into a
smaller period of time. You can save a lot of time with extra effort, but note that if your
real problem is effort, not clock time, that's a very different issue.

3

1. Improve your gym logistics
You can save a lot of time in the gym with circuit training or combo sets. Rather than
perform multiple consecutive sets of the same exercise ('straight sets'), you should hop
from one exercise to the next. The next exercise should train completely different
muscles, because the point is that you let the muscles involved in exercise 1 rest while
you're performing exercise 2.

For example, instead of performing 4 sets of bench presses followed by 4 sets of chin-
ups, you would do set 1 of bench presses, set 1 of chin-ups, set 2 of bench presses, set
2 of chin-ups, etc.

You don't have to use supersets where you don't rest at all in between sets of the
paired exercises though. You should at least catch you breath in between sets.

String as many non-overlapping exercises together as possible. In a crowded gym you
will often want to string together exercises that use nearby equipment, but don't let the
gym lay-out limit you. For example, you can bring a bench and dumbbells to a squat
cage and then you can perform a full-body circuit of squats, chin-ups, bench presses
and gliding leg curls.

Whenever possible, it's best to preserve the order of the exercises in your program, but
it's more important to get your target volume of sets in with reasonable exercises than to
perform the theoretically perfect exercises in the theoretically perfect order. Do try to at

4

least perform all compound exercises involving a certain muscle group before the
isolation work for that muscle group. Unless there's a very long time in between the
isolation and the compound exercise, performing your isolation work first, AKA pre-
fatiguing, will reduce the volume you can complete for all other muscle groups involved
in the compound exercise. For example, if you perform biceps curls before chin-ups,
you will limit the volume done by your back muscles.

5

2. Gradually shorten your rest intervals
By consistently starting your next set just before you're fully recovered, your work
capacity will improve over time so that you will still be able to complete (close to) the
same training volume in progressively less time. If you can perform the same volume,
your results will be the same.

However, you should ideally always rest until you've at least caught your breath in
between sets to avoid turning your strength training into endurance training.

As a general guideline, compound exercises will need at least a minute rest in between
sets and 2 minutes is more realistic for most men. Isolation exercises can often be
performed back-to-back with almost no rest in between sets once your work capacity
has improved.

6

3. Go heavier
While it's counterintuitive, higher intensity training induces less neuromuscular and
subjective fatigue than lower intensity training. With lower rep sets, your muscles
perform considerably less total work per set (i.e. reps x weight) and thereby accumulate
less neuromuscular fatigue. If you think 1RM squats are intensive, try 20RMs. They're
called widow makers for a reason. Lower rep sets also take less time, obviously, so by
going heavier, you reduce the length of your sets and you reduce the subjective fatigue
from the set, allowing you to quickly move to the next exercise.

Note, however, that if you're not performing circuit training or combo sets, going heavier
does not save you time, because it increases the rest interval you need to recover from
that exercise. So going heavier is only time-efficient when you can move to a different
exercise after your set, not if you're doing multiple consecutive sets of the same
exercise.

7

4. Cut sets, not exercises
Due to diminishing returns to volume per exercise, it's better to reduce volume from all
exercises a little than to skip some exercises entirely.

A notable exception to this rule is for exercises requiring an extraordinarily high set-up
time. For these exercises, performing a close substitute is fine. A common example is
the hip thrust from a deficit. For most men, it's a low priority exercise requiring a large
time investment. Performing pull-throughs, (reverse) hyperexensions, bodyweight
unilateral hip thrusts or glute bridges from the floor instead can save a lot of time spent
rearranging benches, loading the bar etc., that could be better spent performing other
exercises.

8

5. When reducing your number of sets, work extra
hard during your remaining sets
With the following techniques, you can save time while still getting enough mechanical
tension on your muscle fibers (at the expense of additional fatigue).

Strategy 1: Progressive partials + eccentric
If you cut 1 set of an exercise, extend the last remaining set with partials in the easiest
range of motion to compensate. You can generally perform considerably more reps if
you progressively shorten your range of motion across the reps. For example, during
bench presses, you can keep grinding out reps if you lower the bar less and less. After
that, if safely possible, perform the slowest possible eccentric rep to a dead stop. During
bench presses or squats, you obviously need safety bars for this to avoid getting pinned
under the weight.

Strategy 2: Myo-reps
If you cut 2 sets of an exercise or the above strategy is not safely possible, perform
myo-reps to reach ~ 80% of your regular number of total reps in however many sets it
takes. Myo-reps consist of a first 'primer' set taken 1-3 reps short of failure, followed by
short rest periods - just catching your breath - also performed 1-3 reps short of failure.
The key is to stay close to failure but never actually fail a rep to stay in the 'most
effective reps' zone. Your myo-rep sets, which are the sets after the first primer set,
headings: 5. When reducing your number of sets, work extra

pages.text:
9

should have 3-5 reps each: adjust your rest interval and proximity to failure to achieve
this.

Example: your reps in a given overhead press workout normally go as follows: 8, 6, 5.
That's 19 total reps, so to shorten that to 1 extended myo-rep set, you should achieve
about 19 x 0.8 = 15 reps. In this case, you could do 6 reps in the first set, catch your
breath, perform 3 more reps, and repeat performing 2 more sets of 3 this way (6 + 3 + 3
+ 3 = 15).

If you can't get to 80% volume in time, extend the last set with partials plus a slow
eccentric as discussed above, if safely possible.

During squats, you can perform breathing squats instead of myo-reps. This means you
'rest' with the bar still on your back and just keep resting and pushing out another rep
until you've gotten in your planned training volume. After this, you don't need to do
anything else anymore (nor will you be able to!)

Strategy 3: No-failure drop sets
When you're really short on time and you have to cut 3 or more sets of an exercise,
perform your first set as normal for as many reps as possible. Then, if safely possible,
perform drop sets with 20% less weight each set for about 5 reps each: you should not

10

intentionally hit failure but also not leave any reps in the tank. Adjust the weight
decrease to ensure you get in 4-6 reps per drop set. Keep performing drop sets until
you've gotten in 80% of your regularly planned total rep number or you get to a weight
below 30% of 1RM, at which point further drop sets wouldn't achieve much anymore.

Example: your normally perform 4 sets of leg extensions that go like 15, 12, 10, 8.
That's 35 total reps, so you want to get in 35 x 0.8 = 28 reps with drop sets. You
perform your first set for 15 reps as normal. You shouldn't need much of a decrease in
weight thereafter to get in 5 more reps, so just put the weight pin down 1 point and keep
going. Depending on how many reps you get this way, decrease the weight 2 more
times to get over 28 reps with sets of ~5 reps.
